([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"): *Sistem Jalan Persekutuan Malaysia*; [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 马来西亚联邦公路系统), is the main national road network in [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). All federal roads in Malaysia are under the purview of the [Ministry of Works](/wiki/Ministry_of_Works_(Malaysia) "Ministry of Works (Malaysia)") (MOW). According to the [Ministerial Functions Act 1969](/wiki/Ministerial_Functions_Act_1969 "Ministerial Functions Act 1969"), the MOW is responsible to plan, build and maintain all federal roads gazetted under the Federal Roads Act 1959.[[3]](#cite_note-3) However, most of the Federal roads' projects were built and maintained by the [Malaysian Public Works Department](/wiki/Malaysian_Public_Works_Department "Malaysian Public Works Department") (Jambatan Kerja Raya, JKR), which is also one of the implementing agencies under the MOW (with the exception of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") and [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), whereby JKR in these two states is under respective state government).

**Malaysian Federal Roads System** ([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"): *Sistem Jalan Persekutuan Malaysia*; [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 马来西亚联邦公路系统), is the main national road network in [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). All federal roads in Malaysia are under the purview of the [Ministry of Works](/wiki/Ministry_of_Works_(Malaysia) "Ministry of Works (Malaysia)") (MOW). According to the [Ministerial Functions Act 1969](/wiki/Ministerial_Functions_Act_1969 "Ministerial Functions Act 1969"), the MOW is responsible to plan, build and maintain all federal roads gazetted under the Federal Roads Act 1959.[[3]](#cite_note-3) However, most of the Federal roads' projects were built and maintained by the [Malaysian Public Works Department](/wiki/Malaysian_Public_Works_Department "Malaysian Public Works Department") (Jambatan Kerja Raya, JKR), which is also one of the implementing agencies under the MOW (with the exception of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") and [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), whereby JKR in these two states is under respective state government).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Federal_Roads_System&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Most of the federal roads in [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia") were built during the [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") colonial era before 1957. At that time, the British government built the roads in order to enable them to transport goods and commodities easier.

In [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah"), most of the federal roads were built during the occupation of [British North Borneo](/wiki/British_North_Borneo "British North Borneo") under [North Borneo Chartered Company](/wiki/British_North_Borneo_Company "British North Borneo Company") administration, and unlike most federal roads in Peninsular Malaysia which uses only numbers to label federal roads, Sabah federal road codes begin with the letter A followed by route number.

However, in [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), no road network system was developed during the rule of [White Rajah](/wiki/White_Rajah "White Rajah") [Brooke](/wiki/Raja_Brooke "Raja Brooke") dynasty. As a result, right after Sarawak joined the federation of Malaysia on 16 September 1963, the federal government of [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") began to build a road network system connecting Sarawak to Sabah, known as [Pan Borneo Highway](/wiki/Pan_Borneo_Highway "Pan Borneo Highway").

## Federal road standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Federal_Roads_System&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Federal road standards")]

### Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Federal_Roads_System&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Overview")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:OOjs_UI_icon_robot.svg) | This section **may incorporate text from a [large language model](/wiki/Wikipedia:LLM "Wikipedia:LLM")**. Any such material should be [removed](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Federal_Roads_System&action=edit). *(February 2026)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

According to Malaysian Road Statistics 2023 by Public Works Department (JKR) Malaysia. The total length of federal roads is 20,017.97 km (12,438.59 mi) and state roads is 285,327.736 km (177,294.435 mi) (Grand total for federal/state roads is 202,198.214 km (125,640.145 mi) as of December 2023, not included local road and rural road under local government authority).[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Federal routes are labeled with only numbers, for example ![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/MYS_Jkr-ft1.svg/40px-MYS_Jkr-ft1.svg.png) [FT 1](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_1 "Malaysia Federal Route 1") (Federal Route 1), while state routes are labeled with the state code letter followed by assigned numbers; for example [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/MYS_blank.svg/40px-MYS_blank.svg.png)](/wiki/File:MYS_blank.svg)[J1](/wiki/Johor_State_Route_J1 "Johor State Route J1") is a [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor") state road. However, federal route numbers can also be added with the
*FT-* prefix, which is normally used by JKR and [Malaysian police](/wiki/Royal_Malaysian_Police "Royal Malaysian Police"). For example, Federal Route 1 can also be written as Federal Route FT1. Both federal and state roads have blue road signs and the text colour is white.

Most of the federal roads in Malaysia are [two-lane roads](/wiki/Single_carriageway "Single carriageway"). Malaysia implements a [right-hand driving](/wiki/Left-_and_right-hand_traffic "Left- and right-hand traffic") system where drivers drive on the left side of the road. However, there are in certain places where additional lanes are available. In town areas, federal roads may become four-lane roads to increase traffic capacity. In hilly areas, additional third climbing lane is available for slower vehicles such as buses and lorries.

Some federal roads may have [motorcycle lanes](/wiki/Motorcycle_lane "Motorcycle lane"). On Malaysian federal roads, the motorcycle lanes are placed at the extreme left side of each direction and only separated from the main lanes by black-and-white stripes to enable motorcyclists to overtake slower motorcycles and to turn right to exit the road.

Some expressways in Malaysia such as [Federal Highway](/wiki/Federal_Highway_(Malaysia) "Federal Highway (Malaysia)") and [Skudai Highway](/wiki/Skudai_Highway "Skudai Highway") are federally funded, therefore all federally funded expressways are also classified as federal roads.

Nearly all federal roads are paved with typical [tarmac](/wiki/Asphalt_concrete "Asphalt concrete") except [Skudai-Pontian Highway](/wiki/Skudai-Pontian_Highway "Skudai-Pontian Highway") which is paved with [concrete](/wiki/Concrete "Concrete") from [Universiti Teknologi Malaysia](/wiki/Universiti_Teknologi_Malaysia "Universiti Teknologi Malaysia") interchange to Taman Sri Pulai junction and [Sitiawan–Batak Rabit road](/wiki/Federal_route_5 "Federal route 5") (Federal route 5) from [Sitiawan](/wiki/Sitiawan "Sitiawan") to [Kota Setia](/wiki/Kota_Setia "Kota Setia"). Meanwhile, at [Federal Highway](/wiki/Federal_Highway_(Malaysia) "Federal Highway (Malaysia)") linking Klang to Kuala Lumpur, the section of the highway from [Subang Jaya](/wiki/Subang_Jaya "Subang Jaya") to [Kota Darul Ehsan](/wiki/Kota_Darul_Ehsan "Kota Darul Ehsan") near [Petaling Jaya](/wiki/Petaling_Jaya "Petaling Jaya") are paved with [asphalt](/wiki/Asphalt_concrete "Asphalt concrete").

Sarawak has some of the most extensive federal road network in Malaysia. All federal roads in Sarawak is connecting main divisions with exception of [Mukah division](/wiki/Mukah_Division "Mukah Division"). As for [Kapit division](/wiki/Kapit_Division "Kapit Division"), the only federal road serving this division is Jalan Bakun (starting from KM 95–KM 120). Coastal road of Bintulu–Miri is a still in dispute between federal government and state government right of maintenance. It is due to the construction is federal funded, but the compensation and acquisition of land are from Sarawak state government. No federal roads are isolated from the network unlike state roads. Uniquely in Sarawak, federal road network is adjoined internationally to Brunei highway at [Sungai Tujuh](/wiki/Sungai_Tujuh "Sungai Tujuh") ([Miri](/wiki/Miri,_Malaysia "Miri, Malaysia")) with [Kuala Belait](/wiki/Kuala_Belait "Kuala Belait") (Brunei), [Tedungan](/w/index.php?title=Tedungan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tedungan (page does not exist)") ([Limbang](/wiki/Limbang "Limbang")) with [Kuala Lurah](/wiki/Kuala_Lurah "Kuala Lurah") (Brunei), Limbang with Puni (Brunei), [Lawas](/wiki/Lawas "Lawas") with Labu (Brunei) and also to Indonesian road network at [Tebedu](/wiki/Tebedu "Tebedu") ([Serian](/wiki/Serian,_Sarawak "Serian, Sarawak") district) with [Entikong](/wiki/Entikong "Entikong") ([Kalimantan Barat](/wiki/Kalimantan_Barat "Kalimantan Barat"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")).

Malaysian federal roads are subject to the rural highway standard adopted by [Malaysian Public Works Department](/wiki/Malaysian_Public_Works_Department "Malaysian Public Works Department") (JKR), ranging from R1 and R1a (minor roads at villages and [FELDA](/wiki/FELDA "FELDA") settlements with no access control and low speed limits) to R5 (federal roads or highways with limited access control and speed limits up to 90 km/h). R6 standard is exclusive for high-speed (up to 110 km/h) [expressways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") with [full access](/wiki/Limited-access_road "Limited-access road") control.

## Asian Highway Network

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_Federal_Roads_System&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Asian Highway Network")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Tabliczka_AH18.svg/250px-Tabliczka_AH18.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tabliczka_AH18.svg)

Asian Highway route shield.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/FT3_AH18.jpg/330px-FT3_AH18.jpg)](/wiki/File:FT3_AH18.jpg)

Federal roads distance sign with Asian Highway route shield.

[Asian Highway Network](/wiki/Asian_Highway_Network "Asian Highway Network") is an international project between Asian nations to develop their highway systems which will form the main routes in the Asian Highway network. There are 7 Asian Highway routes passing through [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") - [AH2](/wiki/AH2 "AH2"), [AH18](/wiki/AH18 "AH18"), [AH140](/wiki/AH140 "AH140"), [AH141](/wiki/AH141 "AH141"), [AH142](/wiki/AH142 "AH142"), [AH143](/wiki/AH143 "AH143"), and [AH150](/wiki/AH150 "AH150").

The Malaysian section of Route AH2 consists of:-

* [North–South Expressway](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_(Malaysia) "North–South Expressway (Malaysia)")
* [New Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/New_Klang_Valley_Expressway "New Klang Valley Expressway")
* [North–South Expressway Central Link](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_Central_Link "North–South Expressway Central Link")
* [Federal Route 1](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_1 "Malaysia Federal Route 1")
* [Johor Bahru Eastern Dispersal Link Expressway](/wiki/Johor_Bahru_Eastern_Dispersal_Link_Expressway "Johor Bahru Eastern Dispersal Link Expressway")
* [Johor Causeway](/wiki/Johor_Causeway "Johor Causeway")

The Malaysian section of Route AH18 consists of:-

* [Federal Route 3](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_3 "Malaysia Federal Route 3")
* [Kuantan Bypass](/wiki/Kuantan_Bypass "Kuantan Bypass")
* [Johor Bahru–Kota Tinggi Highway](/wiki/Johor_Bahru%E2%80%93Kota_Tinggi_Highway "Johor Bahru–Kota Tinggi Highway")

The Malaysian section of Route AH140 consists of:-

* [Butterworth–Kulim Expressway](/wiki/Butterworth%E2%80%93Kulim_Expressway "Butterworth–Kulim Expressway")
* [East–West Highway](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Highway_(Malaysia) "East–West Highway (Malaysia)")
* [Federal Route 4](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_4 "Malaysia Federal Route 4")

The Malaysian section of Route AH141 consists of:-

* [North Klang Straits Bypass](/wiki/North_Klang_Straits_Bypass "North Klang Straits Bypass") and [New North Klang Straits Bypass](/wiki/New_North_Klang_Straits_Bypass "New North Klang Straits Bypass")
* [New Klang Valley Expressway](/wiki/New_Klang_Valley_Expressway "New Klang Valley Expressway")
* [Duta–Ulu Klang Expressway](/wiki/Duta%E2%80%93Ulu_Klang_Expressway "Duta–Ulu Klang Expressway")
* [Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Middle_Ring_Road_2 "Kuala Lumpur Middle Ring Road 2")
* [Kuala Lumpur–Karak Expressway](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur%E2%80%93Karak_Expressway "Kuala Lumpur–Karak Expressway")
* [East Coast Expressway](/wiki/East_Coast_Expressway "East Coast Expressway")
* [Gebeng Bypass](/wiki/Gebeng_Bypass "Gebeng Bypass")

The Malaysian section of Route AH142 consists of:-

* [MEC Highway](/wiki/MEC_Highway "MEC Highway")
* [Tun Razak Highway](/wiki/Tun_Razak_Highway "Tun Razak Highway")
* [Federal Route 1](/wiki/Malaysia_Federal_Route_1 "Malaysia Federal Route 1")

The Malaysian section of Route AH143 consists of:-

* [Second Link Expressway](/wiki/Second_Link_Expressway "Second Link Expressway")
* [Malaysia–Singapore Second Crossing](/w/index.php?title=Malaysia%E2%80%93Singapore_Second_Crossing&action=edit&redlink=1 "Malaysia–Singapore Second Crossing (page does not exist)")

The Malaysian section of Route AH150 consists of:-

* [Pan Borneo Highway](/wiki/Pan_Borneo_Highway "Pan Borneo Highway")
* [Miri–Baram Highway](/wiki/Miri%E2%80%93Baram_Highway "Miri–Baram Highway")
